g.text: Partial Read Problem: Why One recv() Is Never Enough
TCP may deliver any slice — not aligned to HTTP boundary
arrives ~2 ms later — separate IP packet
kernel batched differently on slow network
same wire — same bytes — same arrival pattern
loop stops BEFORE reading this — body boundary
OR: empty path → serves wrong file
OR: silently wrong on large headers
OR: works on localhost, fails on mobile
action | LOOP AGAIN — not done yet
bytes 52-56 | NOT read — loop exited at delimiter
pipelining | those bytes are the NEXT request
correctness | body pointer = buf+52 (body_len=0 for GET)
Works on localhost, mobile, proxy, slow link
Works when Nagle batches headers into 1 segment
Works when MTU splits headers into 20 segments
Works when send() on client side uses tiny writes
No \r\n\r\n visible — parser has no valid terminator
\r\n\r\n at offset 50 — parser has valid terminator
TCP unit | segment (≤ MSS, typically 1460 bytes)
Nagle algo | may COALESCE small writes into 1 segment
IP frag | may SPLIT 1 segment into multiple packets
recv() return | however many bytes kernel has RIGHT NOW
guarantee | NONE on size — only ordering + reliability
The Invariant (binding rule for all TCP parsing)
ALWAYS loop recv() until application-level delimiter found
ALWAYS check return value: n==0 is EOF, n<0 is error
ALWAYS bound the buffer to prevent OOM from malicious input
NEVER assume one send() == one recv()
57-byte request fragmented by TCP into 3 segments
